title: Topic 5: It's Not Cheating If Everyone Does It: The Normalization of Exploits in Competitive Gaming
content:
# Topic 5: It's Not Cheating If Everyone Does It: The Normalization of Exploits in Competitive Gaming

## Abstract

This paper delves into the ethically ambiguous, and often hilarious, world of competitive gaming, where the line between a clever tactic and outright cheating is as blurry as a 144p stream. We explore the normalization of exploits, those delightful little "oopsies" in the game code that players enthusiastically turn into strategic advantages. Through a rigorous, and entirely serious, analysis of player psychology, we examine why a perfectly moral, law-abiding citizen would gleefully abuse a bug that lets them clip through walls and shoot their opponents from the nether realm. We will journey through a landscape of questionable sportsmanship, from the hallowed halls of professional esports to the sticky-floored basements of amateur LAN parties. This paper argues that the normalization of exploits is not merely a technical issue but a profound reflection of human nature, our innate desire to win, and our remarkable ability to justify just about anything if it means getting a shiny new virtual hat.

## Introduction

In the grand theater of human competition, from the ancient Olympics to the modern-day office bake-off, there has always been a simple, guiding principle: may the best person win. But what if the "best" person is simply the one who discovered that you can stand on a particular pixel to become invisible? Welcome, dear reader, to the wild, wonderful, and morally gray world of competitive gaming.

For the uninitiated, an "exploit" in a video game is the use of a bug or glitch in a way that was not intended by the developers, to gain an unfair advantage. It's not quite the same as installing an aimbot, which is the digital equivalent of bringing a gun to a knife fight. No, an exploit is more like discovering that the knife fight is taking place on a trampoline, and no one said you *couldn't* use it to bounce over your opponent's head. It's a loophole, a delightful little quirk of the system that separates the truly dedicated from the merely talented.

This paper will explore the fascinating phenomenon of how these exploits become normalized within gaming communities. We will investigate the psychological drivers that lead players to embrace these morally ambiguous tactics, from the intoxicating allure of victory to the comforting justification that "everyone else is doing it." We will also examine the impact of this normalization on the gaming industry, from the frantic scramble of developers to patch these digital loopholes to the existential crises of esports commentators trying to explain why a player just won a million dollars by walking through a wall. So, grab your popcorn, and let's dive into the glorious mess that is competitive gaming, where the only thing more common than a headshot is a well-timed, game-breaking bug.

## Literature Review

While the hallowed halls of academia have not yet produced a definitive tome on the comedic value of video game exploits, a surprising amount of research has been dedicated to the darker side of gaming: cheating. Scholars have long been fascinated by the question of why people cheat, and their findings provide a fertile ground for understanding the normalization of exploits.

One of the most prominent themes in the literature is the role of motivation. A study by Lee et al. (2021) found that players with high levels of competitive motivation are more likely to engage in cheating. This is hardly surprising. When the desire to win is so strong that it eclipses all other considerations, the line between a clever strategy and a dirty trick becomes conveniently blurred. The researchers also found that aggression is a significant predictor of cheating, which explains why your average Call of Duty lobby sounds like a cage match between a disgruntled wolverine and a particularly angry hornet's nest.

Another key factor is self-esteem. Miller et al. (2007) found that cheaters tend to have lower self-esteem than their more honest counterparts. This suggests that for some players, winning isn't just about the game; it's about validating their self-worth. And if the only way to feel good about yourself is to abuse a bug that lets you see through walls, then so be it. The study also notes that players with high self-esteem are more likely to exhibit good sportsmanship, which is a polite way of saying they're less likely to throw a tantrum when they lose.

The concept of "moral disengagement" is also a recurring theme in the literature. This is the psychological process by which people convince themselves that their unethical actions are actually perfectly acceptable. In the context of gaming, this can manifest in a variety of ways, from blaming the developers for creating a buggy game to insisting that an exploit is a legitimate part of the meta. As we will see, this ability to rationalize our behavior is a cornerstone of the normalization of exploits.

## Analysis

The normalization of exploits in competitive gaming is a complex and multifaceted phenomenon, driven by a potent cocktail of psychological, social, and technological factors. At its core, it is a story about human nature, our insatiable desire to win, and our remarkable capacity for creative problem-solving, even when that problem is "how do I shoot someone through a solid object?"

One of the primary psychological drivers is the concept of **cognitive dissonance**. This is the mental discomfort we experience when our beliefs and behaviors are in conflict. For example, a player might believe that cheating is wrong, but they might also be using an exploit to climb the ranked ladder. To resolve this dissonance, they have two options: stop using the exploit, or change their belief about whether it's actually cheating. Given the choice between giving up a competitive advantage and simply redefining what it means to cheat, it's not hard to see which option many players choose.

This is where **social proof** comes into play. If a player sees that everyone else is using an exploit, it becomes much easier to justify their own use of it. After all, if it's so wrong, why is everyone doing it? This creates a feedback loop, where the more people who use an exploit, the more normalized it becomes, and the more people feel comfortable using it. This is the digital equivalent of the old adage, "If all your friends jumped off a bridge, would you do it too?" In the world of online gaming, the answer is a resounding "yes," especially if there's a rare loot drop at the bottom.

Another key factor is the **ambiguity of the rules**. While some forms of cheating, like using an aimbot, are clearly and explicitly forbidden, the rules around exploits are often much murkier. Is it a bug, or is it an unintended feature? Is it a clever use of game mechanics, or is it a cheap trick? This ambiguity creates a gray area that players are more than happy to exploit, both literally and figuratively. It allows them to maintain a sense of plausible deniability, to tell themselves that they're not really cheating, they're just playing the game in a way that the developers didn't anticipate.

Finally, we cannot ignore the role of the **developers themselves**. In many cases, developers are slow to patch exploits, either because they are difficult to fix or because they are not considered a high priority. This inaction can be interpreted by the community as a tacit endorsement of the exploit, further contributing to its normalization. After all, if the developers didn't want you to use it, they would have fixed it, right?

## Real-World Examples

The history of competitive gaming is littered with a veritable graveyard of game-breaking bugs and hilarious exploits. These incidents, far from being mere footnotes, have often defined entire eras of play, and in some cases, have even led to the downfall of entire games.

One of the most infamous examples is the "coach bug" in *Counter-Strike: Global Offensive*. In 2020, it was discovered that a bug in the game's spectator mode allowed team coaches to gain a god's-eye view of the map, giving them an unfair advantage by relaying enemy positions to their players. Over 30 professional coaches were implicated in the scandal, which sent shockwaves through the esports community. The incident was a stark reminder that even at the highest levels of competition, the temptation to bend the rules can be too great to resist.

Another classic example is the "Jesus shot" from *Tiger Woods PGA Tour 08*. This delightful glitch allowed players to hit a golf ball from the surface of a water hazard, as if by divine intervention. While not a competitive game in the traditional sense, the exploit became a legendary part of the game's culture, a testament to the joy that can be found in breaking the rules.

Of course, no discussion of exploits would be complete without mentioning the infamous case of Nikhil "Forsaken" Kumawat. At a professional *CS:GO* tournament in 2018, Forsaken was caught red-handed using an aimbot. The moment he was caught, frantically trying to delete the cheating software from his computer as tournament officials closed in, has become an iconic image in the esports world. While not an exploit in the strictest sense, the incident is a powerful reminder of the lengths to which some players will go to win, and the public humiliation that can result when they are caught.

## Discussion

The normalization of exploits in competitive gaming is more than just a quirky subculture; it is a fascinating case study in human behavior and consumer habits. It reveals a great deal about our relationship with rules, our desire for status, and our remarkable ability to rationalize our actions.

From a consumer behavior perspective, the normalization of exploits can be seen as a form of **brand co-creation**. Players are not just passive consumers of a product; they are active participants in the creation of the game's culture and meta. They are constantly pushing the boundaries of what is possible, and in doing so, they are shaping the game in ways that the developers never intended. This can be a double-edged sword for game companies. On the one hand, it can lead to a more engaged and dedicated player base. On the other hand, it can create a toxic and unfair environment that drives new players away.

The phenomenon also highlights the importance of **community management**. In the absence of clear rules and enforcement from the developers, the community will create its own norms and standards. This can be a positive thing, but it can also lead to the normalization of toxic behaviors like cheating and exploiting. Game companies that want to cultivate a healthy and welcoming community must be proactive in setting and enforcing clear rules, and in engaging with their players to understand their concerns.

Finally, the normalization of exploits is a powerful reminder of the **importance of fairness**. At its core, a game is a system of rules that is designed to create a fair and enjoyable experience for all players. When those rules are broken, whether through cheating or exploiting, it undermines the integrity of the game and can lead to a great deal of frustration and anger. This is why the issue of cheating is so emotionally charged in the gaming community. It's not just about winning or losing; it's about the fundamental fairness of the competition.

## Conclusion

In the grand, chaotic symphony of competitive gaming, exploits are the unexpected, and often hilarious, sour notes. They are the moments when the carefully constructed rules of the game break down, and human ingenuity, in all its morally ambiguous glory, takes over. As we have seen, the normalization of these exploits is a complex phenomenon, driven by a potent cocktail of psychological, social, and technological factors.

But perhaps the most important takeaway from our journey into this digital heart of darkness is that the line between a cheater and a clever player is often a matter of perspective. It is a line that is constantly being redrawn, not by the developers, but by the players themselves. And as long as there are games to be played, and victories to be won, you can be sure that players will continue to push the boundaries of what is possible, one hilarious, game-breaking exploit at a time.
references:
[1] Lee, S. J., Jeong, E. J., Lee, D. Y., & Kim, G. M. (2021). Why Do Some Users Become Enticed to Cheating in Competitive Online Games? An Empirical Study of Cheating Focused on Competitive Motivation, Self-Esteem, and Aggression. *Frontiers in Psychology*, *12*, 768825. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2021.768825

[2] Miller, C. H., DeMarais, A. A., & Lindstrom, R. A. (2007). The high cost of low self-esteem: A study of cheating in competitive online games. *CyberPsychology & Behavior*, *10*(4), 575-577.

[3] Winkie, L. (2023, January 13). The biggest esports scandals of the past 10 years. *PC Gamer*. https://www.pcgamer.com/the-biggest-esports-scandals-of-the-past-10-years/
